title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
I have a simple question for you. Have you ever thought, how we can find the area of a shape? We know to find the area of a rectangle, we can multiply the length and width. We know the area of a triangle is half of the base times the height. But what if we have a shape that is not a rectangle or a triangle, some weird shape?
Yeah, Yeah, I know, we can use Integration. But the question is, do you understand how integration works? Do you understand the fundamental of integration? If not, then this article is for you. In this article, we will learn about the concept of calculus (Integration / Differentiation) and how it works.






NoteNote



Forget about everything you know about Calculus. Bare with me, and we will re-discover the concept of calculus together.

title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
Let’s try an example
Let’s say we want to find the area under the curve of the function \(f(x) = -x^2 + 5x - 1\) between \(x=1\) and \(x=4\). Then, we can write the area as:
\[
A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{N} f\left(1 + k \frac{4-1}{N}\right) \frac{4-1}{N} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{N} f\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right) \frac{3}{N}
\]
Then, we can find \(f\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right)\) and substitute it back into the summation:
\[\begin{aligned}

f\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right) &= -\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right)^2 + 5\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right) - 1 \\
&= -\left(1 + \frac{6k}{N} + \frac{9k^2}{N^2}\right) + 5\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right) - 1 \\
&= -1 - \frac{6k}{N} - \frac{9k^2}{N^2} + 5 + \frac{15k}{N} - 1 \\
&= 3 + \frac{9k}{N} - \frac{9k^2}{N^2}

\end{aligned}\]
\[
f\left(1 + \frac{3k}{N}\right) \frac{3}{N} = \left(3 + \frac{9k}{N} - \frac{9k^2}{N^2}\right) \frac{3}{N} = \frac{9}{N} + \frac{27k}{N^2} - \frac{27k^2}{N^3}
\]
By substituting it back into the summation, we get: \[
A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left(\frac{9}{N} + \frac{27k}{N^2} - \frac{27k^2}{N^3}\right)
\]
Now, we can split the summation into three separate summations: \[
A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{9}{N} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{27k}{N^2} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{27k^2}{N^3} \right)
\]
Now, we can simplify each summation using the formulas for the sum of the first \(N\) natural numbers and the sum of the squares of the first \(N\) natural numbers:
\[\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{9}{N}
  &= 9 \\

\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{27k}{N^2}
  &= \frac{27}{N^2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} k = \frac{27}{N^2} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)}{2} \\
  &= \frac{27(N+1)}{2N} \\

\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{27k^2}{N^3}
  &= \frac{27}{N^3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} k^2 = \frac{27}{N^3} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)(2N+1)}{6} \\
  &= \frac{9(N+1)(2N+1)}{2N^2}
\end{aligned}\]
We can substitute these back into the expression for \(A\):
\[\begin{aligned}
  A &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \left( 9 + \frac{27(N+1)}{2N} - \frac{9(N+1)(2N+1)}{2N^2} \right) \\
  &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \left(  \frac{18N^2 + 27N^2 + 27N - (18N^2+27N+9)}{2N^2} \right) \\
  &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \left(  \frac{27}{2} - \frac{9}{2N^2} \right)\\
\end{aligned}\]
Finally, we can take the limit as \(N\) approaches infinity: \[
A = \frac{27}{2}
\]
Well, that was easy enough. And this result is infact correct. You can verify it by using integration.






NoteNote



We used squares to approximate the area under the curve, but we can also use trapezoids to get a better approximation. The process is the same, we just need to use the formula for the area of a trapezoid instead of the area of a rectangle. Try it …








TipLesson learned



If we want to find a arbitary shape area, we can devide it to rectangles (or trapezoids), find the area of each rectangle, and sum them up. As we increase the number of rectangles (i.e making the rectangles infinitesimally small), the approximation gets better and better, and in the limit, we get the exact area of the shape.

title: Introduction to Calculus
section: Let’s find the derivative of some common functions
text:
Let’s find the derivative of some common functions

\(f(x) = x^2\)

\[\begin{aligned}
f'(x)
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{(x+\Delta x)^2 - x^2}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{x^2 + 2x\Delta x + (\Delta x)^2 - x^2}{\Delta x} \\
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{2x\Delta x + (\Delta x)^2}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} (2x + \Delta x) \\
&= 2x
\end{aligned}\]

\(f(x) = x^3\)

\[\begin{aligned}
f'(x)
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{(x+\Delta x)^3 - x^3}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{x^3 + 3x^2\Delta x + 3x(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta x)^3 - x^3}{\Delta x} \\
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{3x^2\Delta x + 3x(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta x)^3}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \left(3x^2 + 3x\Delta x + (\Delta x)^2\right) \\
&= 3x^2
\end{aligned}\]

\(f(x) = x^n\)

\[\begin{aligned}
f'(x)
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{(x+\Delta x)^n - x^n}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{x^n + nx^{n-1}\Delta x + \cdots + (\Delta x)^n - x^n}{\Delta x} \\
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{nx^{n-1}\Delta x + \cdots + (\Delta x)^n}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \left(nx^{n-1} + \cdots + (\Delta x)^{n-1}\right) \\
&= nx^{n-1}
\end{aligned}\]

\(f(x) = e^x\)

\[\begin{aligned}
f'(x)
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{e^{x+\Delta x} - e^x}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{e^x e^{\Delta x} - e^x}{\Delta x} = e^x \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{e^{\Delta x} - 1}{\Delta x} \\
&= e^x
\end{aligned}\]

\(f(x) = \sin x\)

\[\begin{aligned}
f'(x)
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\sin(x+\Delta x) - \sin x}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\sin x \cos \Delta x + \cos x \sin \Delta x - \sin x}{\Delta x} \\
&= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \left(
\sin x \cdot \frac{\cos \Delta x - 1}{\Delta x}
+
\cos x \cdot \frac{\sin \Delta x}{\Delta x}
\right) \\
&= \sin x \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\cos \Delta x - 1}{\Delta x}
+ \cos x \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\sin \Delta x}{\Delta x} \\
&= \cos x
\end{aligned}\]
Cool, we have found the derivatives of some common functions. We can use these derivatives to find the area under the curve of these functions using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. So here is a tabule of some common functions and their derivatives and integrals:



Function \(f(x)\)
Derivative \(f'(x)\)
Integral \(\int f(x)\,dx\)




\(c\)
\(0\)
\(cx + C\)


\(x^n\)
\(nx^{n-1}\)
\(\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C,\quad n \neq -1\)


\(\dfrac{1}{x}\)
\(-\dfrac{1}{x^2}\)
\(\ln|x| + C\)


\(\sqrt{x}\)
\(\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\)
\(\dfrac{2}{3}x^{3/2} + C\)


\(e^x\)
\(e^x\)
\(e^x + C\)


\(a^x\)
\(a^x \ln a\)
\(\dfrac{a^x}{\ln a} + C,\quad a>0,\ a\neq 1\)


\(\sin x\)
\(\cos x\)
\(-\cos x + C\)


\(\cos x\)
\(-\sin x\)
\(\sin x + C\)


\(\tan x\)
\(\sec^2 x\)
\(\ln|\sec x| + C\)


\(\sec^2 x\)
\(2\sec^2 x \tan x\)
\(\tan x + C\)


\(\ln x\)
\(\dfrac{1}{x}\)
\(x\ln x - x + C\)

title: Introduction to Calculus
section: Shall we try the same example again?
text:
Shall we try the same example again?
Let’s say we want to find the area under the curve of the function \(f(x) = -x^2 + 5x - 1\) between \(x=1\) and \(x=4\). Then, we can use the integral to find the area:
\[\begin{aligned}
A &= \int_1^4 (-x^2 + 5x - 1) dx \\
&= \left[-\frac{x^3}{3} + \frac{5x^2}{2} - x\right]_1^4 \\
&= \left(-\frac{64}{3} + 40 - 4\right) - \left(-\frac{1}{3} + \frac{5}{2} - 1\right) \\
&= \frac{27}{2}
\end{aligned}\]






TipLesson learned




We can find the area under a curve by approximating it with rectangles and taking the limit as the number of rectangles approaches infinity.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus tells us that integration and differentiation are inverse operations, which allows us to compute integrals much more easily by finding antiderivatives.
Knowing the derivatives of common functions allows us to quickly compute areas under curves without having to go through the entire limit process every time.









ImportantImportant Summary



If we do not know how to find the length, area, volume, any parameter of a changing function, we can segment it into small pieces, which we can easily find the parameter of, and then sum them up. As we make the pieces smaller and smaller, our approximation gets better and better, and in the limit, we get the exact value of the parameter we are trying to find.

title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
Definition: Derivative
Let \(f\) be a function defined on an open interval containing \(x\).
The derivative of \(f\) at \(x\) is defined as
\[
f'(x) =
\lim_{h \to 0}
\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}
\]
provided that this limit exists.
If this limit exists, we say that \(f\) is differentiable at \(x\).

title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
Definition: Riemann Integral
Let \(f:[a,b] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\) be a bounded function.
Divide the interval \([a,b]\) into \(N\) subintervals
\[
a = x_0 < x_1 < x_2 < \ldots < x_N = b
\]
and define
\[
\Delta x_k = x_k - x_{k-1}.
\]
Choose any point \(c_k \in [x_{k-1},x_k]\) and construct the sum
\[
S_N = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f(c_k)\,\Delta x_k.
\]
If the limit
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty} S_N
\]
exists and is independent of the choice of \(c_k\), then \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\) and we define
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx =
\lim_{N\to\infty}
\sum_{k=1}^{N} f(c_k)\,\Delta x_k.
\]

title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
Definition: Indefinite Integral
If \(F'(x)=f(x)\) on an interval, then \(F\) is called an antiderivative of \(f\).
The indefinite integral of \(f\) is the family of all antiderivatives:
\[
\int f(x)\,dx = F(x) + C.
\]

title: Introduction to Calculus
text:
Definition: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

Let \(f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function.

Part I
Define the function
\[
F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt
\]
for \(x \in [a,b]\). Then \(F\) is differentiable on \((a,b)\), and
\[
F'(x) = f(x)
\]
for every \(x \in (a,b)\).


Part II
Let \(F\) be any antiderivative of \(f\) on \([a,b]\), meaning
\[
F'(x) = f(x)
\]
for all \(x \in (a,b)\). Then
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a).
\]
